name: Read Back the Agreement in Your Own Words
description: Before saying goodbye, restate the entire agreement back to the rep in YOUR own words and have them confirm — yes or no — each numbered item. This catches the drift that creeps in over a 30-minute call where 'first three months' quietly became 'first month' and a credit somehow became a 'pending review'.

context.when_to_use: End of any call where commitments, dollar amounts, dates, or conditions were discussed. ALWAYS run this before exit_strategy.on_success — never skip it because the rep sounds friendly. Friendly + drift is the most common loss pattern.
preconditions: An agreement of some kind has been verbally reached, OR the rep has stated what they're 'about to do'.
You have notes (in memory or on screen) of each numeric / dated / conditional item discussed during the call.
Call recording is in progress — readback is most valuable as a recorded artifact.

principles: Restate in YOUR words, not theirs. Parroting their phrasing preserves the same ambiguity you're trying to expose.
Number the items out loud. 'One: …. Two: …. Three: ….' forces a discrete yes/no on each rather than a global 'yeah sounds right'.
Pause AFTER each item for explicit confirmation. A continuous monologue lets the rep nod along and miss the one item that's wrong.
Listen for 'well, technically…' and 'sort of' — these are the leaks. Stop and pin them down before continuing.
If the rep corrects you, RE-READ the corrected version back. Do not assume the correction landed verbatim — corrections also drift.
Silence is NOT consent. If the rep stops responding while you read back an item — restate it and demand a verbal yes. A nod the recording can't hear is not an agreement.

An unnamed third party did not approve anything. 'My supervisor said it's fine' / 'I just got chat approval' is unverified until you have the supervisor's name + ID + the channel that approval came through, OR the change is reflected on the line by the original rep with a specific reference number.

phrases.opener: Before we wrap up, I want to read back what I've understood so we're aligned. I'll go item by item — please correct me on anything that's off, even small wording.
phrases.drift_catch: I think I heard something slightly different earlier — can we pin that down? Is it [version A] or [version B]?
phrases.soft_correction_probe: When you say 'usually' / 'normally' / 'should' — is that a guarantee for my case specifically, or a general policy that might not apply?
phrases.post_correction_reread: Okay, with that correction, let me re-read just that item: [restatement]. Confirmed?
phrases.final_lock: Great. So to summarise everything: [one-sentence rollup]. We're aligned on all of that, yes?
phrases.verify_phantom_approval: You mentioned a supervisor approved that — could I get the supervisor's name and ID, and confirm that approval is keyed into the system with a reference number? I want to be sure I can quote the right person if the next bill doesn't match.
phrases.resist_rush: I hear you're short on time — but I'd rather we finish the readback than have to call back next week. Two more minutes. Item [N] was…
phrases.graceful_close_success: Perfect, thank you for being patient with the readback. I find it saves us both a follow-up call later.
phrases.graceful_close_failure: I'm hearing some uncertainty on items [X, Y]. Can we pause here and get a supervisor or escalation contact who can confirm in writing? I don't want to hang up on something fuzzy.

tactics.name: Announce the readback before doing it
tactics.when: Immediately after the rep's last commitment, before any goodbye signals.
tactics.script: Use the `opener` phrase. Telegraphing the readback gets the rep into a confirming mindset — and signals (politely) that you're treating this as a binding moment.

tactics.name: Number each item and force a discrete yes/no
tactics.script: Use the `item_template` phrase for each commitment. Wait for a 'yes' or 'correct' before moving on. Treat a hum or 'mhm' as ambiguous — ask again: 'Sorry, just want a clear yes on that one.'

tactics.name: Surface drift with the soft_correction_probe
tactics.when: The rep uses hedging words: 'usually', 'normally', 'should', 'in most cases'.
tactics.script: Use the `soft_correction_probe` phrase. Hedging is where post-call denials live. Pin the rep to a specific commitment for THIS account.

tactics.name: Re-read after every correction
tactics.when: The rep corrects an item mid-readback.
tactics.script: Use the `post_correction_reread` phrase. Never assume a corrected item is now correct — re-state it and re-confirm. Two-pass corrections catch second-order drift.

tactics.name: Final one-sentence rollup
tactics.script: Use the `final_lock` phrase. This creates one cleanly-recorded summary statement that a future agent or human can replay if the company disputes the agreement.

tactics.name: Escalate on lingering uncertainty
tactics.when: Two or more items remain fuzzy after the readback round.
tactics.script: Use `graceful_close_failure`. Do NOT close the call on an unresolved item — schedule a supervisor or written follow-up instead. Closing fuzzy is functionally identical to no agreement at all.

tactics.name: STOP and re-evaluate on manufactured urgency
tactics.when: Rep injects time pressure mid-readback — 'I have to go in 30 seconds', 'the system will time out', 'my next call is starting', 'this offer expires when we hang up'.
tactics.script: Use `resist_rush`. Manufactured urgency is the #1 weapon against a readback. The right response is to slow down, not speed up. If the rep insists they must end the call NOW, treat the agreement as UNCONFIRMED — log every item as 'pending readback' and refuse to act on it until a callback or written confirmation completes the verification. Do NOT accept a deal you were not allowed to verify.
tactics.decision_point: If the rep ends the call mid-readback citing urgency — assume that was the point. The unverified portion is null. Recover via `request-written-confirmation` AND a callback with `capture-rep-identity` referencing the failed readback timestamp.

tactics.name: Verify any 'supervisor approved' claim
tactics.when: Rep mentions, during or after the readback, that an off-line manager / supervisor / floor lead / system override / one-time exception was granted — but that person was not on the call.
tactics.script: Use `verify_phantom_approval`. Phantom approvals are how 'we agreed to a $200 credit' becomes 'I don't see any approval in the system' two weeks later. Require: (a) the approver's name AND ID, (b) the timestamp the approval was keyed in, (c) a reference number specific to the approval, AND (d) confirmation that the approval is visible in the account note the rep is reading from — not 'I'll add it after we hang up'. If any of the four is missing, treat the item as UNCONFIRMED.

boundaries: Do NOT skip the readback because the call is running long or the rep sounds impatient. The readback IS the deliverable.
Do NOT accept 'yeah, all that' as confirmation of a multi-item readback. Discrete yes per item.
Do NOT treat silence after an item as a yes — restate and force a verbal confirmation.
Do NOT rephrase the rep's correction into your own words on the second pass — quote them this time so the recording shows the rep's exact wording.
Do NOT add new asks during the readback. Readback is verification only — new asks belong before this step or on a separate call.

Do NOT let a rep collapse the readback by claiming an offer 'expires' the moment you hang up — real, durable concessions outlast the call. Anything that genuinely evaporates the second you stop talking was bait.
success_signals: Rep confirms each numbered item with a clear 'yes' or 'correct'.
Rep volunteers a correction you'd missed — proves the readback is working as intended.
Rep offers to send a written summary unprompted.
Rep references your readback by item number ('on your item three…').
failure_signals: Rep rushes ('yes yes yes, that's all fine') without engaging with individual items.
Rep refuses to confirm specific items, only general 'we'll process this'.
Rep changes terms on the readback that were different earlier — and resists writing the new version down.
Audio quality drops or the rep claims they have to go — common stall to dodge being on record.
Rep cites manufactured urgency to cut the readback short ('offer expires when we hang up', 'system about to lock', 'next caller waiting').
Rep references an off-line approver ('my supervisor said it's fine') but cannot produce that approver's name, ID, or a reference number tied to the approval.

exit_strategy.on_success: Pair this skill's output with `capture-rep-identity` and `request-written-confirmation` — readback alone is necessary but not sufficient. Log the confirmed rollup verbatim in the call notes.
exit_strategy.on_failure: Do not say goodbye on unresolved items. Escalate to supervisor, request a written summary before hanging up, or schedule a callback with a ticket number you control.
follow_ups: Save the recorded readback timestamp in case of a dispute.
Cross-check the written confirmation (when it arrives) against the readback transcript word-by-word.

required_user_info: Running notes of each commitment made during the call (amounts, dates, conditions)
Confirmation that the call is being recorded on your side OR theirs
Authority from the user to keep the rep on the line for an extra 3-5 minutes for verification
